epigraph.text:
The Lord does not look at the things people look at. 
People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the 
heart.

content.text:
Decimals are another way to write fractions—specifically, fractions 
whose denominators are powers of 10. The decimal 0.7 means $\frac{7}{10}$; 
the decimal 0.25 means $\frac{25}{100}$. Understanding this connection 
makes decimals much easier to work with.

The decimal point separates the whole number part from the fractional 
part. Places to the right of the decimal point represent fractions with 
denominators of 10, 100, 1000, and so on.

content.title: Decimal Place Values
content.content:
Reading from left to right past the decimal point:

- First place: **tenths** ($\frac{1}{10} = 0.1$)

- Second place: **hundredths** ($\frac{1}{100} = 0.01$)

- Third place: **thousandths** ($\frac{1}{1000} = 0.001$)

- Fourth place: **ten-thousandths** ($\frac{1}{10000} = 0.0001$)

content.title: Identifying decimal place values
content.problem:
In the number 14.592, identify:

1. The digit in the tenths place

2. The place value of the digit 2

3. The value of the digit 9
content.solution:
1. Moving right from the decimal: 5 is in the **tenths** place.

2. The digit 2 is in the **thousandths** place.

3. The 9 is in the hundredths place, so its value is $9 \times 0.01 = \textbf{0.09}$.

content.title: Reading Decimals
content.content:
1. Read the whole number part (if any).

2. Say "and" for the decimal point.

3. Read the decimal part as a whole number, then say the place value 
 of the last digit.

content.title: Reading decimals
content.solution:
1. "Three and seven tenths"

2. "Twelve and five hundredths"

3. "Four hundred twenty-nine thousandths"

content.title: Writing decimals
content.problem:
Write each as a decimal:

1. Six and three tenths

2. Twenty-five hundredths

3. Eight and forty-seven thousandths

content.title: Decimal to Fraction
content.content:
1. Write the decimal digits as the numerator.

2. The denominator is 1 followed by as many zeros as there are 
 decimal places.

3. Simplify if possible.

content.title: Decimal to fraction
content.problem:
Convert to a fraction in simplest form:

1. 0.6 **(b)** 0.25 **(c)** 2.125

content.title: Fraction to Decimal
content.content:
Divide the numerator by the denominator. The result may terminate (end) 
or repeat.

content.text: Rounding decimals follows the same principle as rounding whole numbers.

content.title: Rounding Decimals
content.content:
1. Find the place you're rounding to.

2. Look at the digit immediately to its right.

3. If that digit is 5 or greater, round up; otherwise, round down.

4. Drop all digits to the right of the rounded place.

content.title: Rounding decimals
content.problem:
Round 3.4567:

1. To the nearest tenth

2. To the nearest hundredth

3. To the nearest whole number
content.solution:
1. Tenths place is 4; next digit is 5, so round up: $\textbf{3.5}$

2. Hundredths place is 5; next digit is 6, so round up: $\textbf{3.46}$

3. Ones place is 3; next digit is 4, so round down: $\textbf{3}$

content.title: The Decimal Point
content.content:
The decimal point as we know it was introduced by John Napier of Scotland 
in 1617 and popularized by Henry Briggs. Before this, various symbols 
were used—Simon Stevin used circled numbers, and some cultures used 
commas (as many European countries still do today). The simple dot became 
standard in English-speaking countries, while the comma is used in much 
of Europe and South America.

marginNotes.text:
And even the very hairs of your head are 
all numbered.

Honest scales and balances belong to 
the Lord.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a lever long enough and a fulcrum on 
which to place it, and I shall move the world.

marginNotes.author: John von Neumann
marginNotes.text:
In mathematics you don't understand 
things. You just get used to them.
